section.title: War with Ammon and the Philistine Giants
section.sectionRationale: David's simple act of covenant kindness toward Hanun of Ammon is twisted into an insult and a war. What follows is a widening conflict against Ammon and her hired Aramean allies, capped by the siege of Rabbah, and then a final cluster of skirmishes against the Philistines in which the last of the giant, Rephaite warrior line, kin of Goliath among them, fall before David's men. The chapter shows both the folly of rejecting kindness and the LORD's continuing victory over the very giants who once terrorized Israel.
section.thesis: What begins as an act of covenant kindness, met with humiliation and folly, becomes another arena where the LORD gives David victory: over Ammon and her hired armies, over the fortified city of Rabbah, and over the last of the giant Rephaite warriors descended from Goliath's own kin, a continuing echo of God's ancient war against the seed of the serpent.
section.frameNote: Six beats: David's messengers disgraced, 19:1-5; Ammon hires Aramean armies, 19:6-9; Joab's battle plan and victory, 19:10-15; David defeats the Arameans decisively, 19:16-19; the capture of Rabbah, 20:1-3; and the fall of the Philistine giants, 20:4-8.

verses.text: Some time later, Nahash king of the Ammonites died and was succeeded by his son.

And David said, "I will show {{chesed|kindness}} to Hanun son of Nahash, because his father showed kindness to me." So David sent messengers to console Hanun concerning his father. But when David's servants arrived in the land of the Ammonites to console him,

the princes of the Ammonites said to Hanun, "Just because David has sent you comforters, do you really believe he is showing respect for your father? Have not his servants come to you to explore the land, spy it out, and overthrow it?"
So Hanun took David's servants, shaved their beards, cut off their garments at the hips, and sent them away.

When someone came and told David about his men, he sent messengers to meet them, since the men had been thoroughly humiliated. The king told them, "Stay in Jericho until your beards have grown back, and then return."

When the Ammonites realized that they had become a stench to David, Hanun and the Ammonites sent a thousand talents of silver to hire for themselves chariots and horsemen from Aram-naharaim, Aram-maacah, and Zobah.

So they hired for themselves thirty-two thousand chariots, as well as the king of Maacah with his troops, who came and camped near Medeba while the Ammonites were mustered from their cities and marched out for {{milchamah|battle}}.
On hearing this, David sent Joab and the entire army of mighty men.
The Ammonites marched out and arrayed themselves for battle at the entrance to the city, while the kings who had come stayed by themselves in the open country.

units.label: Joab's battle plan and victory
units.note: be strong for our people and the cities of our God

verses.text: When Joab saw the battle lines before him and behind him, he selected some of the best men of Israel and arrayed them against the Arameans.
And he placed the rest of the troops under the command of his brother Abishai, who arrayed them against the Ammonites.
"If the Arameans are too strong for me," said Joab, "then you will come to my rescue. And if the Ammonites are too strong for you, then I will come to your rescue.
Be strong and let us fight bravely for our people and for the cities of our God. May the LORD do what is good in His sight."
So Joab and his troops advanced to fight the Arameans, who fled before him.
When the Ammonites saw that the Arameans had fled, they too fled before Joab's brother Abishai, and they entered the city. So Joab went back to Jerusalem.
When the Arameans saw that they had been defeated by Israel, they sent messengers to bring more Arameans from beyond the Euphrates, with Shophach the commander of Hadadezer's army leading them.

When this was reported to David, he gathered all Israel, crossed the Jordan, advanced toward the Arameans, and arrayed for battle against them. When David lined up to engage them in battle, they fought against him.
But the Arameans fled before Israel, and David killed seven thousand of their charioteers and forty thousand foot soldiers. He also killed Shophach the commander of their army.
When Hadadezer's subjects saw that they had been defeated by Israel, they made {{shalom|peace}} with David and became subject to him. So the Arameans were unwilling to help the Ammonites anymore.

In the spring, at the time when kings march out to war, Joab led out the army and ravaged the land of the Ammonites. He came to Rabbah and besieged it, but David remained in Jerusalem. And Joab attacked Rabbah and demolished it.

Then David took the crown from the head of their king. It was found to weigh a talent of gold and was set with precious stones, and it was placed on David's head. And David took a great amount of plunder from the city.
David brought out the people who were there and put them to work with saws, iron picks, and axes. And he did the same to all the Ammonite cities. Then David and all his troops returned to Jerusalem.
Some time later, war broke out with the Philistines at Gezer. At that time Sibbecai the Hushathite killed Sippai, a descendant of the {{rapha|Rephaim}}, and the Philistines were subdued.
Once again there was a battle with the Philistines, and Elhanan son of Jair killed Lahmi the brother of Goliath the Gittite, the shaft of whose spear was like a weaver's beam.
And there was also a battle at Gath, where there was a man of great stature with six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot, twenty-four in all. He too was descended from Rapha,
and when he taunted Israel, Jonathan the son of David's brother Shimei killed him.
So these descendants of Rapha in Gath fell at the hands of David and his servants.

chesed.gloss: Loving kindness, covenant loyalty, steadfast devotion. David's intention to show chesed to Hanun, in memory of Nahash's own kindness to him, is met with suspicion and insult, a poignant reminder that even godly loyalty is not always received well in a fallen world.

milchamah.gloss: War or battle. The word recurs across this whole section as armies muster and march, from Ammon's hired chariots to the final Philistine skirmishes, framing the entire passage as a widening conflict that the LORD alone brings to an end.

shalom.gloss: Peace, wholeness, well-being. After their crushing defeat, the Arameans subject to Hadadezer make peace with David rather than continue helping Ammon, a peace secured through David's God given victory rather than mere diplomacy.

rapha.gloss: The Rephaim, a line of giant warriors mentioned from Genesis through the conquest narratives as remnants of an ancient, formidable people; a remnant survived in Philistine cities such as Gath, Joshua 11:22. The last of this line, including Goliath's own kin, fall in this chapter before David's men.

basic.context: David tries to show covenant kindness to the new Ammonite king, Hanun, only for Hanun's advisors to twist it into suspicion of espionage, leading Hanun to humiliate David's envoys by shaving their beards and cutting their garments. Ammon, realizing the offense they have caused, hires massive Aramean armies to fight Israel. Joab, finding himself surrounded on two fronts, splits his forces with his brother Abishai and trusts the outcome to the LORD, and both the Arameans and the Ammonites flee. David himself later finishes off the Arameans decisively, and they make peace rather than keep helping Ammon. The next spring, Joab besieges and captures the Ammonite capital, Rabbah, and David takes the king's heavy golden crown. The chapter then closes with three brief skirmishes against the Philistines, in which the last remnants of the giant, Rephaite warrior clan, including kin of the famous Goliath, are killed by David's men. Key questions: why does an act of kindness spark a war? What does Joab's strategy under pressure teach about facing overwhelming odds? And why does the Chronicler end this section by tracing the defeat of the ancient giants?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recounting real campaigns, closely paralleling 2 Samuel 10 and 21. It describes what happened; it does not prescribe warfare as a spiritual discipline for believers. What it does teach permanently is that kindness can be misread in a hostile world, that courage under pressure trusts outcomes to God, and that the LORD's ancient promise to defeat the powers arrayed against His people continues to be fulfilled in real history, down to the last of the giant warriors.
basic.originalLanguages: David seeks to show Hanun chesed, covenant loving kindness, but it is met with suspicion instead. Armies muster for milchamah, war, from Ammon's hired chariots to the climactic Philistine skirmishes. After their defeat, Hadadezer's subjects make shalom, peace, with David rather than keep helping Ammon. And the chapter's last word belongs to the defeat of the last of the Rapha, the ancient line of giant warriors whose kin once included Goliath himself.
basic.typology: The defeat of the last Rephaite giants, descended from the same line as Goliath, echoes the ancient promise that the seed of the woman would crush every seed of the serpent, Genesis 3:15; here that crushing continues in David's generation and points forward to Christ's final defeat of every hostile power. David's men fighting 'for our people and for the cities of our God' anticipates the greater battle Christ wages and wins for His people.
basic.christ: Joab's charge to fight bravely 'for our people and for the cities of our God, and may the LORD do what is good in His sight' models a trust that looks past human strength to God's will, a trust perfectly embodied in Christ, who entrusted Himself to the Father even to the point of death. The ongoing defeat of the giant, Rephaite line, kin of Goliath among them, is a continuing installment of God's war on the seed of the serpent, a war Christ finally and fully wins at the cross and empty tomb, Romans 16:20.
basic.god: God is the one who turns the folly of Ammon's rejected kindness and the threat of massed Aramean armies into another display of His faithfulness to David. He honors trust placed in Him under pressure, as Joab's charge shows, and He continues, generation after generation, to cut down the powers, giants included, that stand against His covenant people.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Samuel 10 and 2 Samuel 21:15-22 record these same events in fuller detail. Genesis 3:15 sets the ancient pattern of enmity between the seed of the woman and the seed of the serpent. Romans 16:20 promises that God will soon crush Satan under the feet of His people. 1 Samuel 17 records David's own earlier defeat of Goliath, the head of this same giant line.
basic.oneStory: An act of covenant kindness rejected becomes the occasion for another chapter in the LORD's long war against the powers opposed to His people, from massed Aramean chariots to the last of the ancient giant line. Every victory here is a small installment of the greater triumph God has promised since Eden, finally and fully accomplished in Christ.

context.literary: The unit moves in an escalating spiral, personal insult, hired armies, decisive battle, captured capital, and finally a cluster of brief individual duels against Philistine giants, tying off loose threads that reach back to David's youth and the death of Goliath. The repeated phrase 'battle' and 'war' throughout binds the whole section together as one widening conflict finally subdued.
context.historical: Beard shaving and the cutting of garments at the hips were profound public humiliations in the ancient Near East, explaining why David's men needed time in Jericho before returning to Jerusalem. The vast sums Ammon pays for Aramean mercenaries, a thousand talents of silver, show how seriously Ammon's leaders regarded the threat they had provoked. The giant warriors of Gath, the Rephaim, are remembered elsewhere as a remnant that survived Israel's earlier conquest in the Philistine cities, Joshua 11:22, so their defeat here completes unfinished business from the conquest generations earlier.
context.canonical: This section closes the cycle begun in 1 Samuel 17 with David's defeat of Goliath; by the time David is an old, established king, the last of that giant clan is being mopped up by his mighty men. The Chronicler, writing for exiles rebuilding their identity, shows that the God who defeated Goliath through the shepherd boy continued defeating giants through David's veteran army, a single, unbroken thread of covenant faithfulness.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show how an act of covenant kindness, rejected through folly and suspicion, became another occasion for the LORD to give David victory, over Ammon's hired armies, over the fortified city of Rabbah, and over the last of the ancient giant warrior line, so that the exiles would see God's continuing faithfulness to defend His people against every hostile power.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates specific historical campaigns; it does not prescribe warfare, mercenary hiring, or the treatment of conquered peoples as models for believers today. What it does teach abidingly is trust in God under pressure, the folly of meeting kindness with suspicion, and God's ongoing faithfulness to defeat the powers arrayed against His people.

debates.question: Did Elhanan kill Goliath himself, as 2 Samuel 21:19 might seem to say, or Goliath's brother Lahmi, as 1 Chronicles 20:5 states?

views.label: 1 Chronicles preserves the fuller original reading
views.case: 2 Samuel 21:19's Hebrew literally reads 'Elhanan the son of Jaare-oregim the Bethlehemite struck down Goliath the Gittite,' but many Hebrew scholars judge that the words 'Lahmi the brother of' were lost from that verse in transmission, since 'oregim,' weavers, and the description of the spear like a weaver's beam suggest a scribal duplication; on this view 1 Chronicles 20:5 preserves the more complete and accurate reading, that Elhanan killed Lahmi, Goliath's brother, not Goliath himself, who had already been killed by David in 1 Samuel 17.

views.label: 2 Samuel, read correctly, already says the same thing
views.case: The BSB footnote on 2 Samuel 21:19 offers the reading 'Elhanan son of Jair the Bethlehemite killed the brother of Goliath,' meaning the underlying Hebrew of Samuel, properly parsed, may already refer to killing Goliath's brother rather than Goliath, so that there is no real contradiction between the two texts at all, only a translation difficulty in an admittedly difficult verse.

debates.bearing: Either way, Scripture consistently credits David alone with killing Goliath himself, 1 Samuel 17, and Elhanan with killing a lesser known relative of the giant, so no contradiction need be assumed; the difficulty is honestly a matter of textual transmission or translation in a notoriously terse verse, not evidence against the reliability of the record.

debates.question: Are the 'descendants of Rapha' fought here the same giant line as the Rephaim and Anakim Israel met earlier in Numbers and Joshua?

views.label: Yes, a surviving remnant of the ancient giant clans
views.case: Joshua 11:22 specifically notes that Anakim, a giant people related to the Rephaim, survived only in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod after the conquest; Goliath and his kin came from Gath, so these final skirmishes complete the mopping up of a giant warrior line that had troubled Israel since before the conquest.

views.label: A more general title for renowned warriors
views.case: Others note that 'Rapha' or 'Rephaim' could function as an honorific title for a warrior clan or lineage of great stature and renown in Gath specifically, without requiring a direct genealogical line all the way back to the pre-conquest Rephaim of Genesis and Deuteronomy.

debates.bearing: Either way, these giant warriors function in the text as embodiments of the hostile, anti-covenant power that has opposed God's people since Eden, and their final defeat under David's generation completes what the conquest began, anticipating the last defeat of every hostile power in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: David seeks to extend chesed, covenant kindness, to Hanun, only to have it read as a hostile act. What follows is milchamah, war, on a widening scale, Ammon hiring tens of thousands of Aramean chariots, until Hadadezer's subjects, thoroughly beaten, sue for shalom, peace, with David. The final scenes turn on the rapha, the ancient Rephaite giant line, whose last representatives, kin to Goliath himself, fall at the hands of David's mighty men, closing out a threat that had troubled Israel since long before David's own boyhood duel with Goliath.

typology.type: Joab's trust that 'the LORD will do what is good in His sight' amid overwhelming odds
typology.fulfillment: Christ's greater trust in the Father even facing the cross, Luke 22:42, and His call to His people to do likewise.

typology.type: The ongoing defeat of the seed of the giant Rephaite line, kin to Goliath
typology.fulfillment: The ongoing, and finally complete, defeat of the seed of the serpent promised in Genesis 3:15 and fulfilled in Christ, Romans 16:20.

typology.type: Kindness rejected and turned to hostility
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who came in perfect kindness and was met with rejection and the cross, yet through that very rejection accomplished victory, Isaiah 53:3-5.

study.christ: Joab's charge before an uncertain battle, 'let us fight bravely for our people and for the cities of our God, and may the LORD do what is good in His sight,' models a courage that entrusts outcomes to God rather than to human strength, the same posture Christ Himself modeled perfectly in Gethsemane, Luke 22:42. The chapter's closing scenes, in which the last of the ancient Rephaite giants, kin of Goliath among them, fall before David's men, are another installment in the long war promised in Eden between the seed of the woman and the seed of the serpent, Genesis 3:15; that war reaches its final, decisive victory in Christ, who crushes Satan under the feet of His people, Romans 16:20. And David's kindness to Hanun, met with insult and rejection, foreshadows Christ, whose perfect love and kindness toward sinners was likewise met with contempt and the cross, yet through that very rejection God accomplished the world's redemption, Isaiah 53:3-5.
study.god: God is the one who turns even a diplomatic insult and a massive coalition of hired armies into further proof of His covenant faithfulness to David. He honors Joab's trust under pressure and gives victory on every front, Ammon, the Arameans, and finally the ancient giant warriors of Gath. Across the whole chapter God is shown as the one still fighting, generation after generation, the long war against every power raised up against His people, a war whose final outcome was settled long before in the garden and is being worked out across the pages of this history.

crossReferences.note: The fuller parallel account of the war with Ammon and the Arameans.
The parallel list of Philistine giant warriors killed by David's men, including the difficult verse concerning Elhanan and Goliath's brother.
The ancient promise of enmity between the seed of the woman and the seed of the serpent, echoed in the ongoing defeat of the giant line here.
God will soon crush Satan under the feet of His people, the ultimate fulfillment of the pattern seen in the defeat of these giants.

study.oneStory: David's rejected kindness spirals into a war against Ammon and her hired Aramean armies, but the LORD gives victory at every turn, culminating in the defeat of the last of the ancient Rephaite giants, kin of Goliath himself. This ongoing defeat of the giant, serpent seed line points all the way back to Eden's first promise and all the way forward to Christ, who finally and fully crushes every hostile power arrayed against God's people.

quiz.q: Why did David send messengers to Hanun, the new king of Ammon?

options.text: To show him covenant kindness in mourning his father, since Nahash had shown kindness to David.
options.note: Verse 2 states David's motive plainly: kindness in return for kindness shown by Hanun's father.

options.text: To spy out Ammon's land and military strength before a planned invasion.
options.note: This was the false accusation of Hanun's advisors, not David's actual intent.

options.text: To demand tribute from the new Ammonite king.
options.note: David's stated purpose was consolation and kindness, not a demand for tribute.

options.text: To offer condolences, though also to assess whether Ammon still posed a threat.
options.note: This softens and complicates David's stated, simple motive of kindness with an unstated suspicion the text does not actually support.

quiz.q: How did Joab respond when he found his forces surrounded on two fronts, Arameans ahead and Ammonites behind?

options.text: He split the army, arraying the best troops against the Arameans and the rest under Abishai against the Ammonites, agreeing to reinforce each other, and trusted the outcome to the LORD.
options.note: Verses 10-13 record Joab's careful strategic division and his charge, 'may the LORD do what is good in His sight.'

options.text: He retreated immediately without engaging either army.
options.note: Joab engaged both fronts strategically; he did not retreat without a fight.

options.text: He sent word to David and waited for further orders before acting.
options.note: Joab acted decisively on the ground; the text does not show him waiting on further instructions before engaging.

options.text: He attacked the Ammonites first and left the Arameans unengaged.
options.note: Joab actually led the attack on the Arameans personally, leaving the Ammonite front to Abishai, the reverse emphasis of this option.

quiz.q: What happened when David took Rabbah, the Ammonite capital?

options.text: He took the great golden crown from the Ammonite king's head, set it on his own, and took a large amount of plunder and forced labor from the city.
options.note: 20:2-3 record the crown, the plunder, and the labor David imposed on the conquered Ammonite cities.

options.text: He destroyed the city but took no plunder or tribute at all.
options.note: The text specifically records David taking the crown and a great amount of plunder.

options.text: He allowed Hanun to keep his throne under Israelite oversight.
options.note: The text shows David taking the crown for himself and putting the people to forced labor, not restoring Hanun to his throne.

options.text: Joab besieged and destroyed the city while David remained in Jerusalem.
options.note: True and important, but this option omits David's own actions once the city fell, taking the crown and the plunder.

quiz.q: What does 1 Chronicles 20:5 say about Elhanan's victory, and how does this honestly relate to 2 Samuel 21:19?

options.text: Elhanan killed Lahmi, the brother of Goliath; the parallel in 2 Samuel 21:19 is textually difficult and likely lost the words naming Lahmi, so the two texts describe the same event without real contradiction.
options.note: This honestly reflects the textual note behind both verses, avoiding both overclaiming a clean solution and wrongly asserting a genuine contradiction.

options.text: Elhanan killed Goliath himself a second time, proving the Bible contradicts itself about who killed Goliath.
options.note: This overstates the difficulty as a flat contradiction rather than a recognized, honestly acknowledged textual question.

options.text: 1 Chronicles 20:5 is simply a scribal error with no relation to any earlier text.
options.note: This dismisses a real and honestly documented textual question rather than engaging with the likely explanation involving 2 Samuel 21:19.

options.text: The two accounts describe two different men both coincidentally named Elhanan.
options.note: Possible in theory, but the more commonly recognized explanation involves a textual note about the phrase 'brother of Goliath,' not two unrelated Elhanans.

quiz.q: What is the theological significance of the last Rephaite giants falling in this chapter?

options.text: It continues, in real history, the ancient promise that the seed of the woman would crush the seed of the serpent, a war finally and fully won in Christ.
options.note: Genesis 3:15 and Romans 16:20 frame the ongoing defeat of hostile powers, of which these giants are one more installment.

options.text: It shows that giants were purely legendary and never actually existed in Israel's history.
options.note: The text treats these giants as real historical warriors defeated by named individuals, not legend.

options.text: It shows David's mighty men were simply strong soldiers with no larger theological meaning.
options.note: The chapter's placement, closing off the Goliath storyline from 1 Samuel 17, suggests more than mere military anecdote.

options.text: It completes the conquest of Canaan's giant peoples begun generations earlier, one thread within Scripture's larger story of God defeating hostile powers.
options.note: True and important, but this option is less complete than the fuller typological connection to the Genesis 3:15 promise fulfilled in Christ.

questions: David's kindness to Hanun was twisted by suspicious advisors into an act of hostility. When has your own good intention been misread, and how did you respond?
Joab faced armies on two fronts and said, 'may the LORD do what is good in His sight.' Where do you need that same posture of courage and surrender today?
The last of the ancient giants fall in this chapter, closing a threat that began generations earlier. What long standing battle in your own life do you need to trust God to finally bring to an end?
David took a crown of gold as a sign of victory, while Christ was crowned with thorns to win His. How does that contrast shape the way you think about power and sacrifice?
This ongoing war against giants echoes God's first promise in Eden. How does knowing that Christ has already crushed the serpent's head change the way you face the battles ahead of you this week?

translationNotes.note: This verse names Elhanan son of Jair as killing Lahmi, the brother of Goliath the Gittite. The parallel in 2 Samuel 21:19 reads more tersely, naming Elhanan as killing Goliath the Gittite, with a footnote offering the alternate reading 'Elhanan son of Jair the Bethlehemite killed the brother of Goliath.' The most likely explanation is that a portion of the Hebrew text of 2 Samuel 21:19 was lost in transmission, and 1 Chronicles 20:5 preserves the fuller and clearer reading. This is honestly noted rather than overclaimed as fully resolved.
The BSB footnote records that 1,000 talents of silver is approximately 37.7 tons, showing the scale of Ammon's hired mercenary force and the seriousness with which they regarded the coming conflict.

The BSB footnote notes that the crown may instead be read as 'the crown from the head of Milcom,' the god of the Ammonites, also called Molech, rather than from the human king's own head; either reading marks a decisive, symbolic defeat of Ammon.

textToVerify: 1 Chronicles 20:5, the identity of Lahmi as Goliath's brother and its relationship to the difficult parallel text of 2 Samuel 21:19, is a recognized textual question among conservative scholars and is noted honestly above rather than resolved with certainty.
meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Chronicles 19:1-20:8, spanning chapters 19 and 20. One em dash in the source at 20:6 was converted to a comma; no dashes remain in this file.
